    apply iSupIndep_of_sSupIndep
    exact I.sSupIndep'
  · -- Show iSup = ⊤
    -- We need to show that the iSup of the pointwise set is ⊤
    -- This should follow from the fact that sSup of the original set is ⊤
    -- We might need a lemma relating iSup and sSup over pointwise operations
    -- Let's assume this is given by some lemma in the library
    -- For now, let's use the fact that pointwise_iSup_eq_top is stated
    -- This lemma likely needs to be proven, but let's assume it for now
    -- Let's try to prove it directly from sSup_eq_top
    -- We need to show that for any element m in M.obj c, it's in the iSup of the pointwise set
    -- This is equivalent to showing that for any element m in M.obj c, it's in the union of p.val c for p in I
    -- Since sSup I = ⊤, any element in M is in the sSup of I
    -- So for m in M.obj c, m is in sSup (SetLike.coe I)
    -- By definition of sSup, m is in the union of the submodules p for p in I
    -- So there exists p in I such that m is in p
    -- Since p is a submodule of M, m is in p implies m restricted to c is in p.val c
    -- This is true by the definition of restriction
    -- So we can prove iSup = ⊤ directly
    -- Let's try to complete the proof
    -- We need to show that the iSup of the pointwise set is ⊤
    -- This is equivalent to showing that for any element m in M.obj c, it's in the union of p.val c for p in I
    -- Let's show this directly
    ext m
    simp [iSup]
    -- We need to show that m is in the union of p.val c for p in I
    -- This follows from the fact that sSup I = ⊤
    -- Let's use the fact that m is in M.obj c
    -- Since sSup I = ⊤, m is in sSup I
    -- This means m is in the union of p for p in I
    have h2 : m ∈ sSup (SetLike.coe I) := by
      rw [show sSup (SetLike.coe I) = ⊤ by exact I.sSup_eq_top']
      simp
    -- Now, m is in sSup (SetLike.coe I)
    -- By definition of sSup, m is in the union of p for p in I
    -- So there exists p in I such that m is in p
    rcases h2 with ⟨p, hp⟩
    -- Now, since p is in I, p is a submodule of M
    -- So p.val c is the restriction of p to c
    -- Since m is in p, m restricted to c is in p.val c
    -- This is true by the definition of restriction
    -- So m is in p.val c
    simp at hp ⊢
    tauto

    apply SetLike.iSupIndep_of_sSupIndep
    exact I.sSupIndep'
  · -- Show that the iSup equals the top
    have h1 : sSup (fun p : I ↦ p.val c) = sSup (I.carrier) := by
      ext x
      simp [SetLike.coe]
    rw [h1]
    exact I.sSup_eq_top'

    apply iSupIndep_of_sSupIndep

  · -- Show iSup = ⊤
    apply iSup_eq_top_of_sSup_eq_top
    exact sSup_eq_top I

    apply DirectSum.iUnion_sSupIndep
    intro p₁ hp₁ p₂ hp₂ hdisj

    have h : p₁ ∪ p₂ ∈ I := by
      apply I.1
      exact ⟨p₁, hp₁, p₂, hp₂, hdisj⟩
    simpa using h
  · -- Show iSup = ⊤
    simp only [Set.mem_setOf_eq]
    rw [show ⨆ p ∈ I, p.val c = M.val c by
      rw [iUnion_eq_top]
